video_title: How a Team of Marines Built the Corps' FPV Drone Training Program from a Cold Start
gist: A Marine Corps team at Weapons Training Battalion Quantico describes scaling FPV attack drones from zero fielded in October to over 3,500 today, with projections of ~50,000 by the new year and 100,000+ within two years, riding on a >$1B congressional push and OSD's Drone Dominance Program. Key procurement choices include the DIU blue-listed Nuros Archer drone (~$4-5k each) and Kraken Kinetics' low-voltage arming system that reuses existing C4/blasting-cap DoDICs to avoid standing up new munitions supply chains.

insights.claim: The US Marine Corps had virtually zero first-person-view (FPV) attack drones in October 2024 and, after a top-down tasking, fielded over 3,500 by mid-2026, illustrating how fast a US service can stand up a new drone capability once senior leaders force the issue.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Signals a rapid, service-wide FPV adoption curve that creates near-term demand for drone hardware, arming kits, simulators, and training vendors.
insights.quote: if we were having this conversation in October, we had zero firstperson view attack drones fielded in the United States Marine Corps. Today there's over 3,500 and that number is going up

insights.claim: The Marine FPV effort was directly triggered by Ukraine's December 2024 use of roughly 54,000 FPV attack drones and a January 2025 New York Times article, after which Major General Henderson ordered the team to 'get after it' without long op orders or campaign plans.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Confirms Ukraine's drone volumes are directly reshaping US procurement priorities and timelines, a durable tailwind for FPV-adjacent suppliers.
insights.quote: This is unacceptable that we can't do this and we're going to figure this out and I'm not interested in long op orders and long campaign plans. Get after it.

themes.theme: Drone selection: Nuros Archer and Kraken

insights.claim: The Marine team standardized on the Nuros Archer because it was the only blue-listed FPV drone available at the time, going onto DIU's Blue List on February 14, 2025.
insights.speaker: Chief Warrant Officer (EOD, methods of entry school OIC)
insights.so_what: DIU Blue List inclusion was the gating factor for adoption; vendors seeking Marine/DoD FPV sales must prioritize Blue List certification.
insights.quote: we found the Nuros Archer. The Nuros was the only blue listed drone at the time, FPV drone.

insights.claim: Each fully assembled Nuros Archer FPV drone costs roughly $4,000 to $5,000, and the Corps offsets attrition by flying reusable training payloads and requiring eight rehearsal flights before any kinetic strike.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Per-unit price plus fielding at ~70 per unit and thousands of drones frames the addressable hardware spend; low training attrition limits recurring replacement demand.
insights.quote: the drone itself when you add it all together is somewhere between four and $5,000

insights.claim: The Nuros Archer is a wideband, frequency-hopping radio-frequency drone reported at an ~85% success rate in Ukraine, though Ukraine now fields tens of thousands of cheaper drones per month rather than Archers.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Frequency-hopping RF resilience is a key differentiator against jamming; watch whether cost-per-effect pressure pushes the Corps toward cheaper high-volume drones like Ukraine uses.
insights.quote: Most recent reports at an 85% success rate. They don't have now they're employing tens of thousands of drones per month. So they don't have tens of thousands of archers.

insights.claim: The Marines have flown the fiber-optic Shrike (also known as Sky Cutter), described as one of the best drones in Ukraine, and expect to receive it, signaling movement toward EW-resistant fiber-optic FPV drones alongside RF systems.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Fiber-optic drones are a distinct product category the Corps is adopting to counter electronic warfare; relevant for suppliers of fiber-spool FPV systems.
insights.quote: one of the best drones in Ukraine is called the Shrike, sometimes known as Sky Cutter. And we will receive that drone. We have flown it.

insights.claim: The Corps chose Kraken Kinetics' low-voltage arming system specifically because it reuses munitions already in inventory (C4 and blasting caps with existing DoDIC numbers) rather than requiring a brand-new DoDIC and munition-qualification process.
insights.speaker: Chief Warrant Officer (EOD, methods of entry school OIC)
insights.so_what: Reusing existing DoDICs is the logistics unlock that let the program scale fast; arming-system vendors that avoid new munition qualification have a structural speed advantage.
insights.quote: the best part about this system is it uses doexics that are already in the Marine Corps inventory

insights.claim: The Kraken low-voltage arming system ships completely inert with no energetic hazards, so it can travel on commercial airlines and become kinetic only by drawing C4 and blasting caps from an in-theater ammo supply point.
insights.speaker: Chief Warrant Officer (EOD, methods of entry school OIC)
insights.so_what: Inert transport plus in-theater arming solves the shipboard storage and shipment constraints unique to the Marines, a key differentiator for expeditionary drone-munition designs.
insights.quote: I flew on a commercial airline with this system last week because there's no energetic hazards associated with it. It's completely inert.

insights.claim: The armed Archer's anti-personnel payload disperses 1,272 3mm tungsten cubes and has been described by generals as a 'flying claymore', giving the FPV drone a defined minimum-safe-distance for friendly maneuver.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Quantifies the lethal effect that anchors employment TTPs and payload design; a concrete benchmark for competing warhead offerings.
insights.quote: when that detonates 1,272 3mm tungsten cubid the generals described it as a flying claymore

insights.claim: Because roughly every Marine combat engineer (1371 MOS, one of the Corps' largest specialties) already learns basic electric demolition, arming the drones requires only a slight modification to existing skills rather than new specialist training.
insights.speaker: Chief Warrant Officer (EOD, methods of entry school OIC)
insights.so_what: Leveraging an existing large MOS removes a human-capital bottleneck to scaling, reinforcing why the low-voltage/existing-munitions approach scales fast.
insights.quote: every single 1371 is taught basic demo in their schoolhouse

insights.claim: The team deliberately rejected Ukraine's 'flying IED' approach in favor of an intrinsically safe system, arguing that losing Marines to your own weapon makes a unit less lethal, not more.
insights.speaker: Chief Warrant Officer (EOD, methods of entry school OIC)
insights.so_what: Design philosophy diverges from Ukraine's expendable model, favoring engineered-safe arming devices with selectable fuse settings, a differentiator in US procurement requirements.
insights.quote: For most intensive purposes, what they're doing in Ukraine is building flying IEDs. And that's not what we were going to do.

insights.claim: The electronic safe-and-arming device offers selectable fuse settings (e.g., detonation at 4m, 2m, or on command) so operators can push the frag pattern away from advancing friendly forces, enabling drones to be integrated into combined-arms maneuver rather than static attrition warfare.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Selectable-standoff fuzing is a technical requirement tied to the Corps' need to cross ground rather than hold a no-man's-land, shaping payload/fuze vendor specs.
insights.quote: If you put it on here, it's going to blow up 4 m away from the target. If you put it here, it's 2 m away from the target. Here, it's commanded.

insights.claim: The team codified employment into version 4.1 of a drone pamphlet built over 17 months, with step-by-step billet actions, and reports a 100% success rate with the arming TTPs validated through WERP.
insights.speaker: Gunner Troy Hoteling
insights.so_what: Formalized, validated TTPs are the scaling backbone that lets thousands of Marines employ the system uniformly across the force.
insights.quote: we've TTPed our way to safety

insights.claim: The training model uses a crawl-walk-run pipeline requiring 20 to 35 simulator hours (on JVTE, built with the Office of Naval Research and TCOM, plus DART sims at units) before Marines fly live, with the training payload weighing the same as the anti-personnel payload for aerodynamic fidelity.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: Heavy reliance on simulators fielded at every echelon creates recurring demand for FPV training software and sim setups distinct from drone hardware.
insights.quote: Marines will spend anywhere from a minimum of 20 to upwards of 30 and some

insights.claim: Take-home laptop-and-controller simulators drove the biggest proficiency gains, as the best pilots were those who logged extra sim time on weekends and after hours, a training-access advantage unavailable with live weapons.
insights.speaker: Sergeant Brockup
insights.so_what: Portability of sim training is a decisive skill driver, supporting demand for cheap, distributable FPV sim kits over range-only training.
insights.quote: the best flyers uh through the training we've done have been the guys and gals that have done the simulation time themselves on the weekends or after hours

insights.claim: FPV drone training is vastly more repeatable than legacy anti-armor systems: an infantry battalion sees only about one to four Javelins a year, whereas hundreds of reusable Archer training drones are available for repeated live flights.
insights.speaker: Gunner Troy Hoteling
insights.so_what: Order-of-magnitude higher training reps per Marine implies faster proficiency and higher sustained consumable/spare-parts throughput than traditional missile systems.
insights.quote: a current battalion sees about one to four javelins a year tops

insights.claim: The Marine Corps projects going from over 3,500 FPV attack drones fielded today to roughly 50,000 small lethal drones within about six months and well over 100,000 within two years.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: An explicit demand curve toward 100,000+ drones frames a large near-term procurement opportunity for FPV drone, payload, and arming-kit vendors.
insights.quote: If we're having this conversation around the new year, so you know, 6 or so months from now, we're going to have somewhere around 50,000 small lethal drones. We have this conversation two summers from now, we're going to have well over a 100,000.

insights.claim: The initial per-unit fielding plan was roughly 70 drones (strikes plus hotels), with training distributed across seven regional hubs and over 300 Marines trained centrally at Quantico while 714 were in training at the time of recording.
insights.speaker: Gunner Troy Hoteling
insights.so_what: Per-unit fielding density and hub-based training structure help size total unit-level demand and where recurring training spend concentrates.
insights.quote: the initial fielding plan, I want to say was roughly 70ish, you know, between strikes and hotels per unit

themes.theme: DoD enterprise and Drone Dominance Program

insights.claim: Congress appropriated the department over $1 billion with encouragement to learn from Ukraine and scale drone capability, organized under OSD's Drone Dominance Program run out of DIU (led by 'the honorable West', with Travis Mets as point person).
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: A >$1B funded, DIU-led program is the top-level demand and evaluation gatekeeper; vendors' path to sales runs through its qualifier and Gauntlet events.
insights.quote: Congress gave the department over a billion dollars with the encouragement of figure out how to learn from Ukraine and get these capabilities scale inside the department.

insights.claim: Drone vendors are selected through Drone Dominance Program events: a Camp Grayling, Michigan qualifier drawing dozens of vendors, then a competitive Gauntlet 2 in August where drones are rated on tactical performance by Marines, SEALs, and Rangers, with results posted on a public leaderboard.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: A merit-based, performance-rated competition (not relationships or slideware) is the concrete procurement funnel; leaderboard rankings are a signal for which drone makers win DoD volume.
insights.quote: the top 11 drones at Gauntlet 1 were evaluated by the best Marines in the business, the best SEALs in the business, the best rangers in the business

insights.claim: The Marine team drew heavily on Ukraine, engaging multiple Ukrainian units, running PMEs with analyst Michael Kaufman, consulting former Marines who fought there, and tracking monthly Ukrainian strike reports (drone variant and success rate) to benchmark equipment and training choices.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: US requirements are being continuously calibrated to Ukrainian battlefield data, so tracking which drone variants win in Ukraine is a leading indicator for US procurement preferences.
insights.quote: None of this would exist without them. I mean, they showed that this is possible and this is doable and it's scalable.

insights.claim: The Marines diverge from Ukraine on how explosives are handled even when adopting the same airframes (e.g., the Shrike), because they are directed to train to a different, intrinsically safer arming standard than the Ukrainian method.
insights.speaker: Colonel Scott Cuomo
insights.so_what: US arming/safety requirements differ from Ukrainian practice, meaning foreign-proven drones still need US-specific integration work, a niche for compliant arming-system suppliers.
insights.quote: the Ukrainian method for doing that is different than how we've been directed to train. So, we're working through that.
